strict_win_deltas.delta: P1 keeps `app.use(express.json({verify}))` in its original top-level position, only adding a verify callback, while P2 removes that line entirely and re-adds `app.use(express.json())` only after the new `/webhook` route (using a separate `express.raw()` parser just for that route), splitting the app into two divergent body-parsing strategies.

P2 manually does `body = JSON.parse(req.rawBody.toString('utf8'))` in a try/catch placed after the signature check, so an unparseable body still gets a 401 for a bad signature or a clean 400 JSON `invalid_body` for a good one, whereas P1 relies on the global `express.json()` middleware to parse the body before the route handler (and its signature check) ever runs, so malformed JSON would short-circuit to Express's default error handler instead of P1's own 401/400 logic.

P1's `isValidWebhookEvent(req.body)` shape check runs entirely before `acceptedEventIds.has(req.body.id)`, matching the spec's stated signature→shape→duplicate order, while P2 checks `acceptedEventIds.has(body.id)` before calling `isValidWebhookBody(body)`, so a duplicate id paired with an otherwise-invalid body (e.g. missing `type`) would wrongly return 409 instead of the spec's 400 `invalid_body`.

P1 leaves the single `app.use(express.json(...))` call in its original location so every route automatically inherits JSON parsing, while P2 moves `app.use(express.json())` down to just before `if (require.main === module)` — after `/health`, `/items`, `/items/:id`, and `/webhook` are already registered — leaving a fragile ordering trap for any future route added earlier in the file.
